items.headline: Dana-Farber Phase 2 Trial Demonstrates Durable and Profound Responses to CAR T-Cell Therapy in High-Risk Smoldering Multiple Myeloma
items.preview: Dana-Farber Cancer Institute's CAR-PRISM trial shows promising results for CAR T-cell therapy in early-stage multiple myeloma.
items.article:
In a groundbreaking advancement for blood cancer therapies, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute has presented compelling results from the phase 2 CAR-PRISM clinical trial, heralding a new era in the treatment of high-risk smoldering multiple myeloma (SMM). This investigation uniquely explored the application of Chimeric Antigen Receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy at an earlier, precancerous disease stage, revealing unprecedented durable remissions and deep eradication of malignant cells.
Why this matters: The CAR-PRISM trial's success opens avenues for redefining therapeutic paradigms in smoldering multiple myeloma, suggesting that early, definitive immunotherapy could recalibrate management strategies toward durable remissions or even potential cure.
Over a median follow-up of 15.3 months, all treated patients achieved stringent minimal residual disease (MRD) negativity within two months post-infusion, with sustained MRD-negative status observed and no evidence of disease relapse or mortality. Safety outcomes also underscored the feasibility of this method, with no dose-limiting toxicities or high-grade adverse events reported.

items.headline: Lilly boosts ‘in vivo’ cell therapy capabilities with Kelonia buyout
items.preview: Eli Lilly acquires Kelonia Therapeutics for $7 billion to enhance its in vivo cell therapy capabilities.
items.article:
Eli Lilly is adding new abilities to make drugs that can reprogram cells inside the body, agreeing to acquire privately held biotechnology company Kelonia Therapeutics in a deal worth as much as $7 billion. Lilly will pay $3.25 billion up front and could hand the startup’s shareholders another $3.75 billion if certain milestones are met.
Why this matters: The acquisition positions Lilly at the forefront of a potentially transformative technology with broad platform applicability beyond multiple myeloma, aiming to simplify cell therapy processes and improve patient access to these treatments.
Kelonia’s lead therapy, KLN-1010, is designed to help the body generate immune cells that target BCMA, a protein found on malignant cells, and has shown promising early data in multiple myeloma.

items.headline: STAT+: Finally cracking KRAS as a druggable target
items.preview: New data validate KRAS as a druggable target, offering hope for pancreatic cancer treatment.
items.article:
When Revolution Medicines released results for its drug daraxonrasib in pancreatic cancer patients last week, it finally seemed like there was hope in a condition that has long proven intractable for researchers. This development could be just the start of a wave of new RAS inhibitors.
Why this matters: The validation of KRAS as a druggable target represents a significant breakthrough in cancer therapy, potentially leading to new treatment options for patients with pancreatic cancer and other malignancies.
The need-to-know this morning includes updates from Novo Nordisk on its experimental oral drug for sickle cell disease and Eli Lilly's acquisition of Kelonia Therapeutics.

sources.name: STAT Biotech news: Market-moving coverage of drug development

items.headline: Lab-Grown Mini Brain Models Offer New Hope for Diagnosing and Treating Alzheimer’s Disease
items.preview: Johns Hopkins Medicine researchers develop brain organoids to advance Alzheimer’s diagnosis and treatment.
items.article:
Scientists at Johns Hopkins Medicine have unveiled pioneering research demonstrating the potential of patient-derived brain organoids in advancing Alzheimer’s disease treatment and diagnosis. These intricate, lab-grown clusters of brain tissue represent a groundbreaking platform to explore the disease’s pathology at an unprecedented molecular level.
Why this matters: The ability to generate individualized organoids allows researchers to capture the diversity of Alzheimer’s disease, fostering a more nuanced understanding of disease subtypes and molecular trajectories, which is crucial for developing effective treatments.
The study highlights how these organoids can respond differently to treatments, paving the way for precision medicine in Alzheimer’s therapy, where treatments are customized according to molecular signatures rather than a one-size-fits-all approach.

items.headline: We need to correct the wide-spread omission of equal contribution in article indexing
items.preview: A call to address the omission of equal contribution designations in academic article indexing.
items.article:
The increasing use of ‘equal contribution’ designations reflects the rising importance of collaborative science. However, our system for credit and recognition has not kept pace, leading to inequities in academic careers. Major databases often omit equal contribution notes, distorting career credit and visibility.
Why this matters: This oversight can significantly disadvantage scientists who share equal responsibility for a study’s success, impacting their career advancement and recognition in the scientific community.
To address this, academic profiling platforms should develop features that allow authors to self-report equal contributions, and major indexing entities should enforce compliance with reporting standards to ensure fair recognition of all contributors.
